explanation: The requirement is that before the unit enters the Final state ('finalState'), all parameters must have already been safely stored ('parametersStored'). Since de-energization follows the Final state, ensuring 'parametersStored' holds throughout the Final state is crucial. Thus, we express that whenever 'finalState' holds, it implies that 'parametersStored' is true (that is, all parameters are safely stored whenever the system is in Final state). This precisely captures the safe storage requirement for parameters in the Final state, as stipulated in the natural language.

The property requires that during the Final state (when 'finalState' is true), the 'parametersStored' condition (all parameters safely stored) must always hold. This uses the template 'G (bool_exp2 -> (G bool_exp3))', with 'bool_exp2' as 'finalState' and 'bool_exp3' as 'parametersStored'. This captures the idea that while in the Final state, all parameters are always securely stored until the Final state is completed. The de-energization ('off') is out of scope unless further sequencing is specified (e.g., that parameters must be stored before 'off'); with just 'finalState', this captures the storage requirement during that state.

The requirement is that during Final state, all parameters (if any) are to be safely stored before the unit is de-energized and the system is off. We want to ensure that whenever the system is in Final state, we cannot reach the de-energized state ('off') unless 'parametersStored' is true (parameters were safely stored first). This is a safety property stating: it is always the case that if the system is in the Final state, then being off requires that parameters have been stored. In other words: globally, whenever 'finalState' and 'off' are true at the same time, it must be the case that 'parametersStored' is also true. This is G(((finalState & off) -> parametersStored)). This fits template 30 (G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)), with bool_exp1 = (finalState & off), and bool_exp3 = parametersStored.

The natural language requirement specifies that during the Final state, all parameters (if any) must be safely stored before (and as a precondition of) completion of the final state and de-energizing the unit. We need to ensure that whenever the system is in the 'finalState', it is always the case that 'parametersStored' occurs before the unit goes 'off' (de-energized). More precisely, while in 'finalState', we require that the parameters have been stored ('parametersStored' is true) before or as the system goes 'off'. This can be expressed by requiring that at all times, if 'finalState' holds, then before 'off' holds, 'parametersStored' must hold. This is naturally captured with the LTL formula that requires whenever we are in 'finalState', it is globally always the case that 'parametersStored' holds whenever 'off' is reached. Thus, we use the LTL template 10: G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3), with 'finalState' as bool_exp2 and '(parametersStored -> off)' as bool_exp3. We set N_DURATION to null as this template needs no time bound.

The input requires that during the Final state, all parameters are to be safely stored before the unit is de-energized (off). Here, 'finalState' indicates we are in the Final state, 'parametersStored' indicates all parameters are safely stored, and 'off' indicates the unit is de-energized. The correct semantics is: Whenever we are in the Final state, before 'off' becomes true (i.e., for as long as not 'off'), parametersStored must hold. When 'off' becomes true, the safety requirement is no longer active. This is best captured with the pattern: always, if in Final state and not off, then parametersStored must hold. This is expressed as G(((finalState & (!off)) -> parametersStored)).

The property states that whenever (at every time) the ventilator is in the Final state (finalState), then it must be the case that all parameters (if any) are safely stored (parametersStored) before the final state is complete and the unit is de-energized. Since the provided atomic propositions only include a proposition for when parameters have been safely stored (parametersStored), we use that to encode the requirement. The most natural formalization is to say that always, if we are in Final state, then it must be the case that parameters are safely stored. This is captured by the template 30: G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3), with 'finalState' for bool_exp1 and 'parametersStored' for bool_exp3.

The requirement specifies that during the 'Final state', all parameters must be safely stored before the device is de-energized (i.e., 'off'). In temporal logic, this means that it should never occur that the system is in the 'finalState' and the system transitions to 'off' in the next state, unless 'parametersStored' is true (i.e., parameters were stored before powering off). One standard temporal pattern for this situation is to ensure that whenever we are in the Final state and parameters have not yet been stored, it must not be possible to reach 'off' until 'parametersStored' is achieved. This directly maps to a 'leads-to' or 'until' property: as long as we are in 'finalState' and parameters are not yet stored, 'off' cannot happen. In other words, (finalState & !parametersStored) -> G (!off) until parametersStored occurs. Another way to express this in standard LTL is: G ( (finalState & !parametersStored) -> (!off U parametersStored) ). Using the available templates, template 17 (G (bool_exp2 -> (G bool_exp3))) applies: so, 'when in final state and parameters not stored, always not off', or, more specifically, always: if in finalState and not parametersStored, then G (!off) until parametersStored, which can be approximated as G ((finalState & (!parametersStored)) -> (G (!off))).

The requirement states that during the Final state, all parameters must be safely stored before the final state completes and the unit is de-energized. 'finalState' indicates being in the Final state, 'parametersStored' indicates the parameters have been safely stored, and 'off' indicates the system is de-energized. We want to ensure that whenever we are in the Final state, parameters are stored before the system transitions to the off (de-energized) state. This is a 'response-before' pattern: when in Final state, if 'off' becomes true (i.e., system is de-energized), then 'parametersStored' must have been true at or before that point. This is best captured by the template: G(finalState -> ((!finalState U ((finalState&(X(!finalState)))&(X(parametersStored)))) | G(!finalState))), which ensures that, for every entering of the Final state, before we leave the Final state (which would allow us to eventually reach 'off'), 'parametersStored' must be true. But since we directly have an atomic proposition for entering the 'off' (de-energized) state, we can use a more direct trigger: 'If at some point we leave Final state and immediately turn off (de-energized), in the step when we leave, parametersStored had to be asserted.' This is captured precisely by template 150.

The natural language specifies that during the 'Final' state, all parameters, if any, must be safely stored before the final state completes and the unit is de-energized. Here, 'finalState' indicates the system is in the final state, and 'parametersStored' indicates parameters have been safely stored. The property should require that always, if the system is in the finalState, then the parameters must already be safely stored. This is a global invariant, best captured by 'G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)' where 'bool_exp1' is 'finalState' and 'bool_exp3' is 'parametersStored'. This enforces at all times that if the system is in final state, parameters must be stored, as required by the specification.

The input specifies that during the Final state, all parameters (if any) need to be safely stored before the state is complete and the unit is de-energized. The atomic proposition 'finalState' represents that the current state is Final state, 'parametersStored' indicates parameters are safely stored, and 'off' means the system is completely de-energized. To enforce that parameters are stored before the final state completes (and before de-energization), we require that whenever the system is in the Final state, if de-energization (off) ever happens, it must be after parametersStored becomes true. This is a typical 'response' pattern: whenever in finalState, off implies that parametersStored must have held previously. This can be encoded as: always (if in Final state and off occurs, then parametersStored must have occurred before).

Translating this to LTL using the available templates, the best match is to ensure that in the context of finalState, when off happens, it can only happen if parameters have already been stored. This can be expressed using: G ((finalState & off) -> parametersStored).

Thus: always, if both finalState and off are true, then parametersStored is true (i.e., the parameters have already been safely stored).

(Template 40: G ((bool_exp1 & bool_exp2) -> bool_exp3)). This captures the required causality within the final state just before de-energization.
